Knee Osteoarthritis, Left Unilateral primary osteoarthritis, left knee (M17.12) Plan: Counseling - Knee DJD. Viet A Nguyen, PA-C (Primary Provider) Desert Inn Page 1 Russell T Nevins, MD (Bill Under) 2800 East Desert Inn Road (702) 731-1616 Work Ste 100 (702) 734-4900 Fax Las Vegas, NV 89121-3609 DESERT DOC ORTHOPAEDIC CENTER Baker, Thomas Experience Excellence Visit Note - March 10, 2026 PMS ID: Sex: DOB: Phone: MRN: 114700PAT000060216 Male 01/29/1955 (310) 961-6900 MM0000059703 Musculoskeletal care: I advised the patient that most patients with degenerative joint disease of the knee can achieve some measure of pain relief from nonsurgical options like medications, lifestyle modification, exercise and physical therapy, and use of supportive devices or change in footwear. The importance of maintaining an ideal body weight or losing weight to reduce stress on the knee joint is a cornestone of nonoperative management. Medication Counseling NSAIDS : I discussed with the patient that NSAIDs should be taken with food. Prolonged use of NSAIDs can result in the development of stomach ulcers or bleeding. Patient advised to stop taking NSAIDs if abdominal pain occurs. The patient verbalized understanding of the proper use and possible adverse effects of NSAIDs. All of the patient's questions and concerns were addressed. OTC Recommendations: NSAIDs: Discussed Meloxicam for pain. Take with food. If medication helps he will speak with his PCP about long term use. Active Therapy : Active therapies include stretching exercises, weight training (quadriceps strengthening), and cardiovascular non-impact conditioning. Weight reduction may be emphasized as appropriate. Passive Therapy : Passive therapies include ice, heat, massage, ultrasound, and electrical stimulation. Your therapist will decide which of these interventions will be most beneficial to you. Surgical Options and Alternatives Intraarticular steroid injection : I discussed with the patient that this involves the injection of a potent antiinflammatory substance (cortisone) into the knee joint. Risks include whitening of the skin at the injection site and a transient rise in blood glucose. Complications are extremely rare; infection is the most common. There is no rule as to how many injections can be given. Because some research has shown that too much cortisone can damage cartilage, most physicians limit the number of shots they will give you. Intraarticular viscosupplementation : I discussed with the patient that this procedure involves the injection of artificial joint fluid into the knee. The fluid acts as a lubricant, shock absorber, and an antiinflammatory agent. Depending on the specific agent used you will receive one to five shots, each shot one week apart. This intervention is most effective if used in early stage osteoarthritis. Side effects include knee swelling and pain at the injection site. Long term efficacy is not currently known. Complications include but are not limited to infection and joint swelling. Total knee replacement : I explained to the patient that this is an option for patients that have severe pain in their knee from arthritis who have failed conservative management. The diseased parts of the knee are replaced with metal and plastic. Approximately 90% of patients who have total knee replacement experience a significant reduction in pain and a dramatic improvement in their ability to perform common activities of daily living. High impact activities should be avoided after surgery, if possible, to prolong the life of the implant. Serious complications occur in less than 2% of patients. Risks include but are not limited to infection, injury to blood vessels and nerves, blood clots, heart complications, lung complications, stroke, and stiffness. Diagnostic Imaging Results I reviewed and discussed the results of the X-Ray(s) with the patient. After counseling the patient, we decided on the following plan for the LEFT KNEE: Total knee replacement 2. Knee Osteoarthritis, Right Unilateral primary osteoarthritis, right knee (M17.11) Plan: Counseling - Knee DJD. Musculoskeletal care: I advised the patient that most patients with degenerative joint disease of the knee can achieve some measure of pain relief from nonsurgical options like medications, lifestyle modification, exercise and physical therapy, and use of supportive devices or change in footwear. The importance of maintaining an ideal body weight or losing weight to reduce stress on the knee joint is a cornestone of nonoperative management. Intraarticular viscosupplementation : I discussed with the patient that this procedure involves the injection of artificial joint fluid into the knee. Diagnostic Imaging Results I reviewed and discussed the results of the X-Ray(s) with the patient. Follow up PRN for: Further Evaluation and Management - (with Physician). Other Instructions: will schedule for pre-op appt once medically cleared, with DR. Nevins Note: Patient presents for bilateral knee follow up. He has end stage arthritis of bilateral knees. He would like to proceed with total knee replacement. I discussed with him that given his current minimal pain, the goal of the surgery should be to give him more functionality in his knees. I explained to him that there are no guarantees with total knee replacement, and that he may have more pain following knee replacement which he fully understands. His biggest complaint with his knees is that during periods of standing that it becomes difficulty to move again and issues going up and down stairs. Understanding the risks, we will proceed with total knee replacement. He will follow up with Dr. Nevins for pre-operative evaluation.
